title: Claude Code Remote Control
text:
Claude Code’s Remote Control feature lets you control a Claude Code CLI session running in a terminal from a web browser. This means you can:

Start a Claude session on a powerful machine (or a remote server)
Connect to it from your phone, tablet, or any browser
Approve tool calls, provide input, and monitor progress remotely

title: Claude Code Remote Control
text:
Claude Code’s Remote Control feature lets you control a Claude Code CLI session running in a terminal from a web browser. This means you can:

Start a Claude session on a powerful machine (or a remote server)
Connect to it from your phone, tablet, or any browser
Approve tool calls, provide input, and monitor progress remotely

title: Claude Code Remote Control
text:
Prerequisites







Requirement
Details




Claude Code CLI
Latest version installed (claude command available)


Anthropic Plan
Max plan (Pro being rolled out)


Network
Outbound HTTPS only — no port forwarding needed


Browser
Any modern browser on any device

title: Claude Code Remote Control
text:
Quick Start

Option 1: Start a New Remote Session
claude --remote-control
This starts Claude Code and immediately displays:

A URL you can open in any browser
A QR code you can scan with your phone



Option 2: Convert Existing Session
If you already have a Claude Code session running:

Type /remote-control (or /rc for short) in the Claude Code prompt
The URL and QR code appear in your terminal
Open the URL in your browser



Option 3: Enable for All Sessions
claude config set --global remote_control true
This makes every future Claude Code session start with remote control enabled automatically.

title: Claude Code Remote Control
text:
Step-by-Step Setup

Step 1: Start Claude Code with Remote Control
# Navigate to your project
cd ~/Code/nano/nano-meme

# Start with remote control
claude --remote-control
You’ll see output like:
Remote control URL: https://claude.ai/rc/abc123...
Scan the QR code or open the URL above in your browser.

█████████████████████████
█████████████████████████
████ ▄▄▄▄▄ █ ...  ████
...


Step 2: Connect from Browser
Open the URL on any device. You’ll see a web interface that mirrors your terminal session, allowing you to:

Read Claude’s responses
Approve or deny tool calls (file edits, bash commands)
Type messages to Claude
Monitor progress



Step 3: Work Remotely
Once connected, the browser interface provides full control. The terminal continues running — you can close the browser tab and reconnect anytime using the same URL (as long as the terminal session is still alive).

title: Claude Code Remote Control
text:
Persistent Sessions with tmux
For long-running or headless sessions (e.g., on a VPS), combine remote control with tmux:
# SSH into your server
ssh user@your-server.com

# Start a tmux session
tmux new -s claude-work

# Start Claude with remote control
cd ~/projects/my-app
claude --remote-control

# Detach from tmux: Ctrl+B, then D
# The session keeps running!
Later, reconnect:
ssh user@your-server.com
tmux attach -t claude-work
The remote control URL remains valid as long as the tmux session is alive.

title: Claude Code Remote Control
text:
SSH from Desktop App
If you use the Claude Desktop app, you can SSH into a remote machine:

Open Claude Desktop
Start a new conversation
Use the terminal integration to SSH into your server
Run claude --remote-control on the remote machine

title: Claude Code Remote Control
text:
Important Limitations







Limitation
Details




Terminal must stay open
If the terminal process exits, the remote session ends


Network timeout
~10 minutes of network inactivity may disconnect (reconnect via same URL)


One browser at a time
Only one browser can be connected to a session


No file upload
You can’t upload files through the remote interface


Approval still needed
Tool calls still require your approval unless auto-approved

title: Claude Code Remote Control
text:
What Can Be Automated
From the setup steps above, here’s what Claude Code can do itself vs. what requires manual action:








Step
Automated?
Notes




Install Claude Code CLI
Manual
Requires npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code


Navigate to project
Automated
Claude can cd to the project


Start claude --remote-control
Requires session restart
Current session must end and restart with the flag


Enable global config
Automated
claude config set --global remote_control true


Generate QR code
Automatic
Displayed by Claude Code on startup


Open browser URL
Manual
User must scan QR or click URL


Approve tool calls
Manual
User approves via browser interface









ImportantSession Restart Required



To enable remote control for the current session, the Claude Code process must be restarted with the --remote-control flag. This cannot be enabled mid-session unless using the /rc slash command.

title: Claude Code Remote Control
text:
Recommended Workflow
For NanoMeme development, the ideal remote control setup is:
# Terminal 1: Start persistent session
tmux new -s nanomeme
cd ~/Code/nano/nano-meme
claude --remote-control

# Scan QR code with phone
# Now you can monitor and approve from your phone
# while Claude works on the codebase

# Detach: Ctrl+B, D
# Reattach later: tmux attach -t nanomeme
This lets you walk away from your desk while Claude continues working — approving tool calls from your phone when needed.

title: Mobile Architecture
text:
mobile/
├── app/                              # Expo Router screens
│   ├── _layout.tsx                   # Root layout (Tabs + QueryProvider)
│   ├── index.tsx                     # Home: generate meme
│   ├── result.tsx                    # Result: view + share
│   ├── gallery.tsx                   # History of generated memes
│   └── settings.tsx                  # API key, resolution, about
├── src/
│   ├── features/meme/               # Domain logic
│   │   ├── geminiTypes.ts            # API request/response types
│   │   ├── geminiClient.ts           # REST API client
│   │   └── memeService.ts            # Generate + save orchestrator
│   ├── hooks/
│   │   └── useSecureStorage.ts       # API key in expo-secure-store
│   ├── stores/
│   │   ├── memeStore.ts              # Zustand: generation state + history
│   │   └── settingsStore.ts          # Zustand: user preferences
│   └── utils/
│       ├── colors.ts                 # KaosMaps brand tokens
│       └── constants.ts              # App config, API URLs, defaults
├── global.css                        # Tailwind imports
├── tailwind.config.js                # KaosMaps theme
├── app.json                          # Expo config
├── eas.json                          # EAS Build profiles
└── jest.config.js                    # Multi-project Jest config

title: Mobile Architecture
Mobile Architecture
text:
Testing Architecture
Two Jest projects handle different test needs:



Project
Runner
Scope




unit
ts-jest
Pure TS: utils, stores, services, API types


components
jest-expo
React Native components (TSX)



This separation avoids jest-expo’s heavyweight setup for pure logic tests, keeping the test suite fast (~2 seconds for 49 tests).

title: State Management
State Management
State Management
State Management
text:
Design Decisions

Zustand v5 (not Redux, not Context)









Criterion
Zustand
Redux Toolkit
React Context




Boilerplate
Minimal
Moderate
None


Performance
Selective re-renders
Needs useSelector
Re-renders entire tree


Bundle size
~2KB
~30KB
0 (built-in)


DevTools
Optional middleware
Built-in
None


Persistence
Middleware
Middleware
Manual





Two Stores (not one)
Separation prevents unnecessary re-renders. Changing resolution doesn’t cause the home screen’s generation UI to re-render, because they subscribe to different stores.

title: NanoMeme Compendium
text:
NanoMeme is a meme generator experiment using Google’s Nano Banana 2 model (gemini-3.1-flash-image-preview, released February 26, 2026). It produces two deliverables:

macOS Swift menu bar app — a hyper-composable SPM monorepo
React Native + Expo smartphone app — iOS + Android with NativeWind

Both apps call the Gemini REST API directly, render text overlays in-app, and share the KaosMaps brand design language.






NoteAcceptance Criteria



A high-fidelity image of a Yoda-like character with the text “Make or make not. There is no buy” — generated, rendered, and displayed in both apps.

title: Brand Design & Color Palette
Brand Design & Color Palette
text:
Light Mode Contrast Optimization
The default KaosMaps green (#22c55e / green-500) has a WCAG contrast ratio of only 2.1:1 against white backgrounds — failing AA standards. Our light theme uses optimized alternatives:



Color
Default
Optimized
Contrast vs White




Green
#22c55e (500)
#15803d (700)
4.5:1 (AA pass)


Cyan
#06b6d4 (500)
#0891b2 (600)
4.6:1 (AA pass)


Teal
#14b8a6 (500)
#0d9488 (600)
4.5:1 (AA pass)


Pink
#ec4899 (500)
#be185d (700)
5.2:1 (AA pass)









WarningWCAG AA Minimum



Text must have a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1 against its background. The optimized colors above all meet this threshold for light mode while staying visually connected to the brand palette.

The signature KaosMaps glow combines layered shadows and subtle borders:

layer?.shadowOffset = .zero

title: NanoStorageKit — SwiftData Persistence
text: NanoStorageKit handles all data persistence: meme records in SwiftData, export to clipboard/file/share, and user settings.

title: NanoStorageKit — SwiftData Persistence
text: NanoStorageKit handles all data persistence: meme records in SwiftData, export to clipboard/file/share, and user settings.

title: NanoStorageKit — SwiftData Persistence
text:
MemeRecord
A SwiftData @Model for storing generated memes:
@Model
public final class MemeRecord {
    public var prompt: String
    public var overlayText: String
    public var resolution: String
    public var aspectRatio: String
    public var imagePath: String
    @Attribute(.externalStorage) public var thumbnailData: Data?
    public var createdAt: Date
    public var isFavorite: Bool
}






TipExternal Storage for Thumbnails



The @Attribute(.externalStorage) directive tells SwiftData to store thumbnail data as an external file rather than inline in the database, improving query performance.

title: NanoStorageKit — SwiftData Persistence
NanoStorageKit — SwiftData Persistence

Method
API

Copy image to system clipboard

Save to user-chosen location


Share

System share sheet

title: SPM Monorepo Architecture
text:
Package.swift
The root manifest uses Swift tools version 6.0 with language mode v5 (for compatibility with libraries that aren’t fully Swift 6 ready):
// swift-tools-version: 6.0
import PackageDescription

let package = Package(
    name: "NanoMeme",
    platforms: [.macOS(.v14)],
    products: [
        .library(name: "NanoMemeLib", targets: ["NanoMeme"]),
        .executable(name: "NanoMeme", targets: ["NanoMemeApp"]),
    ],
    dependencies: [
        .package(path: "Packages/NanoCore"),
        .package(path: "Packages/NanoDesignKit"),
        .package(path: "Packages/NanoImageKit"),
        .package(path: "Packages/NanoStorageKit"),
        .package(url: "https://github.com/sindresorhus/KeyboardShortcuts", from: "2.0.0"),
    ],
    targets: [
        .target(
            name: "NanoMeme",
            dependencies: ["NanoCore", "NanoDesignKit", "NanoImageKit",
                          "NanoStorageKit", "KeyboardShortcuts"],
            swiftSettings: [.swiftLanguageMode(.v5)]
        ),
        .executableTarget(name: "NanoMemeApp", dependencies: ["NanoMeme"]),
        .testTarget(name: "NanoMemeTests", dependencies: ["NanoMeme"]),
    ]
)






NoteWhy .swiftLanguageMode(.v5)?



Swift 6’s strict concurrency checking conflicts with KeyboardShortcuts’ static lazy initialization pattern. Using v5 language mode gives us the SPM 6.0 features while keeping concurrency warnings non-fatal.

title: NanoCore Package
App Layer — UI + Composition
text:
The central observable state object using Swift’s @Observable macro:
@Observable
public final class AppState {
    public var isGenerating = false
    public var generationProgress: Double = 0
    public var currentMeme: NSImage?
    public var currentPrompt = ""
    public var currentOverlayText = ""
    public var selectedResolution: Resolution = .one
    public var isAPIKeyConfigured = false
    public var lastError: String?
}
SwiftUI views automatically react to changes in these properties without explicit bindings.

PopoverView
A SwiftUI view with three states:


Show code

    Idle --> Generating : tap Generate
    Generating --> Result : success
    Generating --> Idle : error / cancel
    Result --> Idle : Generate Another





stateDiagram-v2

    Idle --> Generating : tap Generate
    Generating --> Result : success
    Generating --> Idle : error / cancel
    Result --> Idle : Generate Another






The view features KaosMaps dark theme styling with cyan glow accents:

Dark surface background (#0a0a0f)
Cyan-bordered input fields with subtle glow
Pulsing generate button during generation
Image preview with copy/save/share actions

Keyboard Shortcuts
Two global shortcuts registered via the KeyboardShortcuts library:



Shortcut

Toggle popover visibility

title: Build Pipeline
text:
The entire build lifecycle is managed by a justfile with these recipes:
# Debug build
build:
    swift build

# Build and run debug binary
run: build
    .build/debug/NanoMeme

# Run all tests
test:
    swift test

# Release build (arm64)
build-release:
    swift build -c release

# Create .app bundle from release binary
bundle: build-release
    # Copy binary to .app/Contents/MacOS/
    # Copy Info.plist, set version and git SHA
    # Copy AppIcon.icns if available

# Ad-hoc codesign the .app bundle
sign: bundle
    codesign --force --deep --sign - \
        --entitlements Resources/entitlements.plist \
        --options runtime \
        "build/NanoMeme.app"

# Install to /Applications
install: sign
    cp -R build/NanoMeme.app /Applications/

# Full dev cycle: kill + rebuild + install + launch
reinstall:
    pkill -f NanoMeme || true
    just install
    open /Applications/NanoMeme.app

title: macOS Menu Bar App
text:
The macOS app is a menu bar application (no Dock icon) that lives in the system tray. Click the photo.artframe icon to open an NSPanel popover with the KaosMaps glow design.
The architecture follows the nano-speak pattern: a two-target SPM project with local packages for maximum testability and composability.

title: macOS Menu Bar App
text:
Key Design Decisions

Two-Target Pattern
The NanoMemeLib library target contains all logic, while NanoMeme is a thin @main entry point. This allows the test target to import the library without importing AppKit lifecycle code.
// NanoMemeApp.swift — the entire executable target
import AppKit
import NanoMemeLib

@main
struct NanoMemeApp {
    static func main() {
        let app = NSApplication.shared
        let delegate = AppDelegate()
        app.delegate = delegate
        app.run()
    }
}


LSUIElement
The app sets LSUIElement = true in Info.plist, which means:

No Dock icon
No main menu bar
Lives entirely in the system tray
Uses NSApp.setActivationPolicy(.accessory) at runtime



Actor-Based Concurrency
All network and image operations use Swift’s structured concurrency. GeminiClient and ImageGenerator are Sendable final classes with async methods, not actors, to avoid executor deadlocks with URLSession.






TipLesson Learned



Using actor + DispatchSemaphore.wait() causes a deadlock because the semaphore blocks the actor’s executor, preventing the URLSession completion handler from running. Solution: use async/await with URLSession.data(for:).

NanoImageKit is the core domain package handling all image generation. It contains:

Gemini API types and client

Image generation orchestration

Gemini API Reference
text:
API Key Security








Platform
Storage
Mechanism




macOS
Keychain
Security.framework with service ID


Mobile
SecureStore
expo-secure-store (iOS Keychain / Android Keystore)


Fallback
Environment
GEMINI_API_KEY env var (macOS only)



Both platforms encrypt API keys at rest using hardware-backed security. Neither stores keys in plain text (no UserDefaults, no AsyncStorage).

text: Both the macOS Swift app and the Expo mobile app share common design language, prompts, and API integration patterns. This section documents the shared foundations.

Architecture Parallel


Show code
graph TB
    subgraph macOS [macOS Swift]
        SC[GeminiClient.swift] --> SM[GeminiModels.swift]
        IG[ImageGenerator.swift] --> SC
        IG --> TO[TextOverlayRenderer]
    end

    subgraph Mobile [Expo React Native]
        TC[geminiClient.ts] --> TT[geminiTypes.ts]
        MS[memeService.ts] --> TC
        MS --> AI[AI Prompt Text]
    end

graph TB
    subgraph macOS [macOS Swift]
        SC[GeminiClient.swift] --> SM[GeminiModels.swift]
        IG[ImageGenerator.swift] --> SC
        IG --> TO[TextOverlayRenderer]
    end

    subgraph Mobile [Expo React Native]
        TC[geminiClient.ts] --> TT[geminiTypes.ts]
        MS[memeService.ts] --> TC
        MS --> AI[AI Prompt Text]
    end

    API[Gemini REST API] --> SC
    API --> TC






Both platforms call the same Gemini REST endpoint, use the same Codable/TypeScript types, and handle the same error codes. The key difference is text overlay: macOS renders text via Core Graphics post-generation, while mobile includes text in the AI prompt.

Mobile App (Expo + React Native)
text:
The mobile app is built with Expo SDK 55 and React Native 0.83, targeting both iOS and Android from a single codebase. It uses:

Expo Router v55 for file-based tab navigation
NativeWind v4 (Tailwind CSS for React Native) with KaosMaps dark theme
Zustand v5 for state management
TanStack Query v5 for API call caching
Lucide React Native for icons (no emojis in UI)
expo-secure-store for encrypted API key storage

title: UI Screens
UI Screens
text:
Result Screen
Displays the generated meme with share/save/favorite actions.

KaosMaps Glow Effect
The image container features the signature KaosMaps glow:
glowOuter: {
  position: "absolute",
  top: -2, left: -2, right: -2, bottom: -2,
  borderRadius: 18,
  shadowColor: colors.brand.cyan,
  shadowOffset: { width: 0, height: 0 },
  shadowOpacity: 0.15,
  shadowRadius: 30,
},
glowInner: {
  position: "absolute",
  top: -1, left: -1, right: -1, bottom: -1,
  borderRadius: 17,
  borderWidth: 1,
  borderColor: "rgba(6,182,212,0.3)",
},


Actions



Action
Icon
Library




Share
Share2
expo-sharing


Save
Download
Alert confirmation


Favorite
Heart
Zustand toggleFavorite

title: UI Screens
text:
Gallery Screen
FlatList of generated memes with the MemeCard component.

MemeCard Component
Card (row layout)
├── Image (80x80 thumbnail)
├── Content
│   ├── Overlay text (1 line, truncated)
│   └── Date
└── Actions
    ├── Heart (toggle favorite)
    └── Trash2 (delete)
The gallery uses keyExtractor on item.id and renders with gap: 12 spacing between cards.


Empty State
When no memes exist, a centered message encourages the user to start generating.

title: UI Screens
text:
Settings Screen
Three sections: API Key, Generation defaults, and About.

API Key Section
The API key input features a secure text field with eye toggle:
apiKeyRow
├── TextInput (secureTextEntry toggled)
└── Eye/EyeOff toggle button

apiKeyActions
├── "Save to Keychain" button (cyan)
└── "Remove" button (red, conditional)






NoteSecureField Pattern



The secureTextEntry prop is toggled via the eye icon, allowing users to verify their API key before saving. The key is stored in expo-secure-store which uses iOS Keychain / Android Keystore.




Generation Defaults

Resolution picker: 4 options (0.5K, 1K, 2K, 4K) — horizontally laid out
Aspect ratio picker: 3 options (1:1, 16:9, 9:16) — horizontally laid out
Auto overlay text: Switch toggle with cyan track color



About Section
Simple key-value rows showing app name, version, model (Nano Banana 2), and author (KaosMaps). Separated by subtle cyan-tinted dividers.
